tasks.title: Spreadsheets, Airtable, or Database? When to use each.
tasks.notes: ## When to migrate from spreadsheets to Airtable- You have spreadsheets which reference data in other spreadsheets- You get anxious about breaking something when you touch the spreadsheet.- You regularly count or filter rows- You track workflows (status, assignments, follow-ups)# Airtable -> SQL* 50k-100k rows* Performance slows* Your data needs get more complex* Want to connect data to external systems* Need custom validation logic* Data is mission-critical, and losing it would be *really bad** You have the technical staff to Do you have spreadsheets which reference data in other spreadsheets? Once you get more than 1 or 2 of these cases, it makes sense to migrate to Airtable.

tasks.title: Answer core Sales Funnel Questions
tasks.notes: - [ ] What problem do you solve for customers?    - Overly expensive technical solutions from unaccountable vendors.    - Difficulty getting the attention of people who could benefit the most from the organization.    -  - [ ] What will your customer's life look like if they buy your product?	- Save time otherwise spent wrangling data.	- Increase the impact of your organization's mission.	- Get the message you want to the people who need to hear it.- [ ] What consequences does your product help customers avoid?    - Vendor lock-in    - Unaccountable vendors without a stake in the community    - Low-quality solutions liable to break- [ ] What does somebody need to do to buy your product ("Click *buy* now?" "Call today?")

Update Pricing Section
tasks.notes: Look into changing things up if you're still uncertain about pricing. You could give some examplese of prices charge, while making it clear that an actual price would need quote. This at least allows for some anchoring.Three accordion/HTML Detail sections for each distinct service, which has distinct pricing models. We don’t offer hourly or ad-hoc consulting. We’ve found that clearly scoped engagements lead to better outcomes for both sides.**Web Scraping***Pricing covers data acquisition and delivery in an agreed format. Analysis, dashboards, or application integration are separate.*Simple: Structured data, easily accessed, not in the format you want it. Limited to a small number of sources and runs.  Example -- downloading and processing CSVs. $1,000Intermediate:  You have to click around to get the data, and it's not structured. $2,500-$3,000.Complex: The constraints aren't clear and you're not sure how much data you're getting or what format it's in: Quote. All can either be deployed once, or maintained actively, with maintenance discussions a quote.**Web App**Phase 1: Paid Discovery and Design: $2,000Phase 2: Scope Blocks, each $4000.*Each block delivers a prioritized set of features agreed on at the start of the block. New requests replace existing scope, or roll into a future block.*Phase 3: Ongoing Maintenance. Quote**Ongoing Support**$1500 / month for:- Ongoing stewardship of your system, priority responses to issues, monitoring, small fixes, and general technical guidance.- Monitoring, small fixes, and questions- Priority response for issues- Does not include major new features, redesigns, or large migrations.Includes up to ~20 hours/month. Larger initiatives are scoped separately.

Website Revisions
We're A Good Fit For You If...
tasks.notes: • You want systems that your team can understand, maintain, and extend over time• You care about long-term clarity and reliability more than quick, opaque fixes• You’re comfortable being involved in technical decisions rather than fully outsourcing them• You want documentation and explanations alongside working software• You value progress that reduces risk and dependency, not just short-term output• You want the freedom to continue with us *or* transition the work elsewhere without frictionWe work best when problems are real, constraints are visible, and decisions can be made as we learn. Most engagements start small, focus on clarity, and grow only if it makes sense to do so.**We’re probably not a good fit if:**• You want a black-box solution you don’t have to think about• You prefer a vendor relationship built on long-term dependency• You’re looking for proprietary systems that only one team can maintain• You want speed at the expense of understandability or future flexibility

tasks.notes: Per Debbie Stine:- **"You do a lot of things [all over the place]"**    - Right now, you're more of a generalist. I looked at your LinkedIn profile.    - Your headline is very general. It would be useful to be a bit more specific.    - Your about section is pretty good.    - You don't really have a mission, so to speak.        - I have a pitch that I'm trying to help science and engineers use their scientific knowledge to make the world a better place.        - You kind of have that because you say you're working for nonprofits, local governments, but you don't have a purpose            - To help them communicate, make things better.        - In your second paragraph, you mention your skills, but it's not clear to people how you can help them achieve their goals.    - (I talk about my stake in Pittsburgh, and how that might work as a brand)        - (She likes)        - I wouldn't just limit yourself to Pittsburgh, you can include Southwest Pennsylvania (and people are sensitive to that) or Western Pennsylvania.        - So saying you focus regionally on Southwestern Pennsylvania, West Virginia, and Southwestern Ohio.    - I do career coaching. And a lot of times I help people with their LinkedIn pages, and getting across to them what it is that you do.    - On your page, you have that you're a software consultant, but that's a contract, but you don't have your firm. It should be that this is a project within your firm, otherwise it looks like you're working for them full-time as a software consultant. If somebody was looking at you.    - The projects themselves are interesting, and you would mention this in your about section, which is currently very technical. You need to add a social goal.        - Police Data also looks interesting.    - There's a section where you talk about projects, and connect to your projects. If you create something that's publically accessible.    - LinkedIn is a very iterative process.        - I spend a lot of time looking at LinkedIn profiles.

tasks.title: Add Pro Bono Page
tasks.notes: In addition to paid software engineering work, I also provide pro bono software engineering services to small nonprofits and community organizations in the City of Pittsburgh.My availability for pro bono work is considerably more limited than my availability for paid work, and paid clients do receive priority in terms of attention. If the work is time-sensitive or requires high availability, I would recommend my paid offerings.In some cases, I can also direct people to other tech workers in the City of Pittsburgh willing to perform pro-bono work.

tasks.title: Values
tasks.notes: The thing about values is they raise the threshold for what things will stop them from doing something.I note often that people will disguise priorities by making reference to how busy they are. Someone will say they can't go to the gym, for example, because they're too overwhelmed with other things. That's partially true, but disguises the part where they are deciding to prioritize those other things OVER going to the gym.People will tell themselves stories that make their decisions seem like inevitable consequences of forces beyond their control.People make decisions far more often than they realize or are comfortable admitting

tasks.title: Build productization entry points
tasks.notes: When someone asks, *“So how do you usually work with orgs?”*You want a **tight 20-second answer**.Examples:- 2 week data pipeline audit- Legacy system modernization assessment    - Where are you using tech in a way that slows you down? Where could it be improved? Are you using a bunch of excel spreadsheets and need to migrate to a database?- Fractional backend lead for 1-2 days/week.

tasks.title: Should your small nonprofit use GenAI?

Build one-liners
tasks.notes: Composed of three parts:1. Probelm2. Solution3. Result"When people struggle with too many excel spreadsheets, I upgrade their data organization and save them time."- "If your data is spread out among countless spreadsheets, I upgrade it and save you time and make it easier to analyze.""When people want to communciate their data to an audience, I build web apps that visualize that data."- "If you want to communicate data to an audience, I build web apps that do that.""If orgs have public data they want to analyze but don't have the time to collect it, I scrape it, and save them time."- "If you have public data that'd too long to gather, I scrape it.""When small organizations need a software engineer but can't afford or don't need to hire one full-time, I step in to fill the gap."- "You know how many organizations want to do more sophisticated technical stuff, but they don't have enough work or the budget to justify hiring a software engineer full time? Well I'm a fractional software engineer. ### ### The Problem- The hook that gets people paying attention- Stating the problem...    - ...is a way to be remembered in your customer's mind    - ...adds perceived value to the product#### Exercise- State the problem or pain point most of your clients face that you can solve    - Name only one problem and make it the one most people feel.        - You can talk about other problems in other parts of the sales funnel    - Think about how you are different from your competition, and problems your competition creates with their services### ### The solution- Connect the solution directly to the problem.- Close the story loop.- Don't over-explain your solution, and don't get cute or clever.#### Things To Consider- Keep it simple- Use your name in the solution#### Exercise- Talk about your solution to the problem you just stated.

tasks.title: Find nonprofit publications to stay up to date
How leaderboards can drive volunteer engagement
tasks.notes: I think it depends strongly on the nature of the activity and how (or if) those activities are already recorded. For the Farm, for example, where part of the point is low-friction, come in and come out, and off-screens, it doesn't make sense. On the other hand, for something like Safe Sidewalks, which requires an hour-long training, and where the data is already being logged digitally, a leaderboard is more sound.

What do you think about converting address in memory to census tract and storing that instead of address?
tasks.notes:
Pittsburgh has ~140 census tracts (~2,000-4,000 people each). Precise enough for district boundary lookups, but less identifying than an address or coordinates. Can't be reversed to street address. What the Census Bureau uses for redistricting. 



Could be useful if and when districts change (approximately once every 10 years) or if we ever want to add new districts beyond school board and city council.

LinkedIn Post for blog post
tasks.notes:
Some of you have already noticed, but I have started my own consultancy, @Lemali Consulting!

As part of my work, I'll also be writing blog posts about my perspective on the tech needs of Pittsburgh's nonprofit and local government, as well as about community building.

My first post, which I'll link below, is about why being embedded in the community you're doing business with matters. Being embedded in the community gives you insight and a genuine investment in solving that community's problems. Just as importantly, there are social consequences to doing a poor job, because you interact with people in that community every day, who will remember whether you did right by them or not. External vendors do not have those incentives, and that's part of why external vendors routinely underserve organizations in Pittsburgh and other communities.

A big thanks to @Sarah SanGiovanni, The Director of Research at the @SWPA Municipal Hub, for providing me the lovely picture of Millvale for this post.

(and then post a link in the comments)
